title: Energy balance in nonsmall cell lung carcinoma patients before and after surgical resection of their tumors
abstract:
717
Energy Balance in Nonsmall Cell Lung Carcinoma
Patients before and after Surgical Resection of Their
Tumors
BACKGROUND. The purpose of this study was to investigate whether surgical re-
Elisabeth W. H. M. Fredrix, Ph.D.1,2
moval of a tumor inﬂuences energy balance, body weight, and body composition
Agnes J. Staal-van den Brekel, M.D.2
in lung carcinoma patients.
Emiel F. M. Wouters, M.D., Ph.D.2
METHODS. In 53 nonsmall cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) patients, resting energy
expenditure (REE, measured by ventilated hood), energy intake (EI, determined
1 Faculty of Natural Sciences, Open University,
by diet history), body weight, and body composition (fat free mass [FFM], measured
Heerlen, the Netherlands.
by bioelectrical impedance analysis) were all determined before tumor resection.
2 Department of Pulmonology, University Hos-
In 39 of 53 patients, REE, EI, body weight, and body composition were also mea-
pital Maastricht, Maastricht, the Netherlands.
sured 3, 6, and 12 months after tumor resection.
RESULTS. Thirty-six of 53 patients (68%) were found to be hypermetabolic. Four-
teen patients were excluded from the repeated measurements. Patients with cura-
tive tumor resection (n Å 30) showed an increase in body weight over a 1-year
period, in contrast to patients with tumor recurrence (n Å 9), who lost weight
(/3.5 vs. 03.6 kg, P õ 0.005). The weight gain was caused predominantly by an
increase in fat mass (FM), while the weight loss was caused for more than half by
a decrease in FFM. Body weight was increased in hypermetabolic patients (n Å
20) as well as patients with normal metabolism (n Å 10) 1 year after successful
removal of their tumors. However, although EI/REE was signiﬁcantly increased in
hypermetabolic patients (from 106% to 140%, P õ 0.05), it was not changed in
patients with normal metabolism.
CONCLUSIONS. Hypermetabolic NSCLC patients undergoing curative resection
show an improvement in energy balance caused by both a decrease in REE and
an increase in EI. This positive energy balance results in weight gain, which is
caused predominantly by an increase in FM. Cancer 1997; 79:717–23.
q 1997 American Cancer Society.
KEYWORDS: lung carcinoma, energy expenditure, dietary intake, body composition,
tumor resection.

Energy Balance in Nonsmall Cell Lung Carcinoma
Patients before and after Surgical Resection of Their
BACKGROUND. The purpose of this study was to investigate whether surgical re-
Elisabeth W. H. M. Fredrix, Ph.D.1,2
moval of a tumor inﬂuences energy balance, body weight, and body composition
Agnes J. Staal-van den Brekel, M.D.2
Emiel F. M. Wouters, M.D., Ph.D.2
METHODS. In 53 nonsmall cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) patients, resting energy
expenditure (REE, measured by ventilated hood), energy intake (EI, determined
1 Faculty of Natural Sciences, Open University,
by diet history), body weight, and body composition (fat free mass [FFM], measured
by bioelectrical impedance analysis) were all determined before tumor resection.
2 Department of Pulmonology, University Hos-
In 39 of 53 patients, REE, EI, body weight, and body composition were also mea-
pital Maastricht, Maastricht, the Netherlands.
sured 3, 6, and 12 months after tumor resection.
RESULTS. Thirty-six of 53 patients (68%) were found to be hypermetabolic. Four-
teen patients were excluded from the repeated measurements. Patients with cura-
tive tumor resection (n Å 30) showed an increase in body weight over a 1-year
period, in contrast to patients with tumor recurrence (n Å 9), who lost weight
(/3.5 vs. 03.6 kg, P õ 0.005). The weight gain was caused predominantly by an
increase in fat mass (FM), while the weight loss was caused for more than half by
a decrease in FFM. Body weight was increased in hypermetabolic patients (n Å
20) as well as patients with normal metabolism (n Å 10) 1 year after successful
removal of their tumors. However, although EI/REE was signiﬁcantly increased in
hypermetabolic patients (from 106% to 140%, P õ 0.05), it was not changed in
patients with normal metabolism.
CONCLUSIONS. Hypermetabolic NSCLC patients undergoing curative resection
show an improvement in energy balance caused by both a decrease in REE and
an increase in EI. This positive energy balance results in weight gain, which is
caused predominantly by an increase in FM. Cancer 1997; 79:717–23.
